ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Pong Piah; mapped to broader pastry food product class. Pong Piah is a specific Singaporean Chinese baked wheat-flour pastry filled with maltose, sesame, and onion.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Pong Piah; A00LC (biscuits, crackers and similar baked products) is the closest defensible broader category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pong Piah is one piece weighing approximately 30 g, providing around 123 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Pong Piah — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pong Piah (CR ID: F11110020). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical Singapore source at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); GI values cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Pong Piah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy to baked wheat-flour pastries and biscuits with sugar and fat content comparable to Pong Piah, as documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 18.18) / 100 ≈ 9.5 for a 30 g serving (18.18 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 448 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; high sugar (22.9 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.8 g/100 g) further promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Pong Piah due to its elevated sodium content (448 mg/100 g) and high refined sugar load, both of which contribute to blood pressure elevation; if consumed, restrict to one small piece (≤30 g) on rare occasions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 22.9 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate at 60.6 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 1.8 g/100 g triggers the refined carbohydrate gate (>50 g/100 g carb with fibre <3 g/100 g), producing rapid postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Pong Piah or limit intake to a single small piece (≤30 g) on rare occasions, as its high sugar and refined carbohydrate content with minimal fibre will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting with fresh fruit or unsalted nuts is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g from palm oil and possible lard/shortening exceeds the 5 g/100 g CVD threshold; high sodium (448 mg/100 g) and high refined sugar (22.9 g/100 g) compound cardiovascular risk through dyslipidaemia, hypertension, and endothelial inflammation pathways.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Pong Piah given its combination of elevated saturated fat, high sodium, and high refined sugar, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles, blood pressure, and vascular inflammation; occasional consumption should be limited to one small piece (≤30 g).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pong Piah (蓬饼) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese baked pastry of Hokkien and Teochew heritage, commonly found in Singapore's Chinese confectionery shops and festive food stalls. It is made from wheat flour dough enriched with palm oil or lard, filled or layered with a sweet mixture of maltose, sugar, onion, and sesame seeds, then baked until golden and lightly crisp. The texture is characteristically flaky and slightly chewy, with a sweet, malty, and faintly savoury flavour profile imparted by the sesame and onion. Per 100 g, Pong Piah provides 410 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 15.8 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g from palm oil), 60.6 g carbohydrate, 22.9 g sugar, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 448 mg sodium. Its high sugar and refined carbohydrate content with low fibre makes it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category) by analogy to similar baked wheat-flour pastries, but the high carbohydrate density yields a meaningful glycemic load per serving. Pong Piah is typically consumed as a snack or festive treat and is not considered a health food; portion control is essential for all population groups with metabolic risk factors.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F11110020) at confidence 0.95, which is the canonical primary source for Singapore food composition. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source and is clearly documented as such.
notes: saturated_fat_g of 6.5 g/100 g is estimated from palm oil and likely lard/shortening content as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat; this is consistent with the fat profile of similar baked pastries. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pong Piah and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific traditional Singaporean pastry as of 2026. Halal status is uncertain as traditional recipes may use lard; consumers requiring halal-certified products should verify with the specific manufacturer. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on baked wheat-flour pastries with comparable sugar and fat content per Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for Pong Piah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
